text: The surgeon enters the retroperitoneal space by grasping the peritoneum over the left lateral pelvic sidewall. Using an ultrasonic energy instrument, meticulous hemostasis is maintained for a clear surgical field. The sigmoid colon is gently dissected away from the peritoneum by wiping its fatty tissue, utilizing fatty tissue planes as safety zones. A grossly dilated ureter is visible, exhibiting peristaltic activity parallel to the left ovarian vascular pedicle. The left ovarian artery runs parallel to the ureter, resembling the internal iliac artery. The endometriotic nodule responsible for ureteral compression is exposed as surrounding tissues are dissected. Further dissection of the hydroureter is performed by spreading and advancing pararectal fat tissue, using fat tissue as a safeguard to separate the ureter from the rectum.

The surgeon enters the endometriotic nodule and drains its contents, using traction and counter-traction to separate surrounding tissues. A surgical gauze is used to clean the surgical field, avoiding cautery or irrigation. Dissection continues in the pararectal fatty tissue using a push-and-spread technique, attempting to mobilize the rectum away from the ureteral obstruction site.

The surgeon applies traction on the median umbilical ligament, exposing the obliterated umbilical artery and confirming it is not the internal iliac artery parallel to the ureter. Further dissection liberates the ureter from surrounding fibrotic tissue, visibly improving poststenotic dilatation. The ovarian remnant containing the endometriotic nodule is fully freed from adhesions.

The surgeon mobilizes the distal end of the ureter until it crosses over the uterine artery. Fibrotic attachments surrounding the ureter are excised and removed using a grasping forceps and tissue dissector, freeing the ureter along its distal course. Care is taken to avoid damaging the ureter's adventitia during the meticulous removal of fibrotic bands.

The surgeon excises ovarian remnant tissue around the endometriotic nodule using an ultrasonic energy instrument. The ureter is fully mobilized up to the junction beneath the uterine artery, and further freed from underlying tissue attachments. After relieving external compressions, the ureter visibly regains its anatomical continuity.
